# Guillermo Rauch’s Post
5mo
Vercel is 𝘰𝘧𝘧𝘪𝘤𝘪𝘢𝘭𝘭𝘺 10 years old, according to the State of Delaware, the first US 🇺🇸 state 🫡. Some reflections from the journey: 1️⃣ You can just incorporate things "In America, they let you start companies easily". It's magical how fast you can get your ', 𝙸𝚗𝚌', your bank account, your LOIs, your payroll set up… Docusigns fly, investors are eager to take risk, there's an overwhelming sense of optimism around technology. Business owners are the heroes of this society. We must preserve this company-building culture at all costs. Thankful to San Francisco for being the epitome of it. 2️⃣ 10 years is the baseline It's wild to me that it feels like Vercel is just getting started. I remember prior to starting the company looking at the statistics on the median time from inception to IPO for tech companies. It was 8-10 years then and now looking like 10-14. There are many tough moments while building a company. But what's a bad day or quarter going to do if you know this is a 15-year journey? 3️⃣ It's always day zero When I registered the domain name and shipped the first company landing page, there was no Next.js. No Git integration. No CDN. No Turborepo. No AI SDK. No v0. At one point, Jamstack and static sites were all the rage. Now, agents and AI. The only way to survive in this game is to have tremendous adaptability and appetite for shipping. Our first name was "Zeit", because the only way to win is to capture the Zeitgeist. As Malte Ubl likes to say, iteration velocity and shipping is the solution to all known s̶o̶f̶t̶w̶a̶r̶e̶ ̶e̶n̶g̶i̶n̶e̶e̶r̶i̶n̶g̶ problems in the world.
I really like this viewpoint. It's always day 0 .no company is too big to change and innovate if there is a culture to do that . Failure is part of life !

# How Vercel boosts AI and human performance with context
This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
6mo Edited
It's now well understood that the better the context, the better the performance of AI models. But it's the same lesson as scaling human organizations. Maximize and enrich context. Engineers and designers are not code & pixels pushers. They solve business problems. At Vercel we've found that maximizing transparency and a 'build in public' ethos increases autonomy (agency 😁) and makes work more fulfilling and impactful. This is incidentally how we end up with stories of interns engineering infrastructure improvements that save millions upon millions of dollars for our customers: vercel.fyi/stampede

Alexander Obenauer has a new series on the need for composability in operating systems. His latest is https://lnkd.in/e4aTFTK4 He's a good thinker about this stuff. And I resonate with the mission. Unlike Obenauer I suspect, I am much more wedded to text than graphics and graphic design. I love text interfaces, and one reason for that is that I just think it's much easier to do all the other good things like abstraction and composition with text than it is in a graphical environment. The Unix pipeline has just worked for decades. There are millions of ad hoc "applications" created on the fly, every day, by people piping together some Unix tools. It's so good you don't notice it. Composing GUI elements is much harder. You can kind of embed one thing in another by having one app allocate a canvas to act as a window to another. These days, it's not uncommon to use iframes in web pages to compose from multiple sources. But full integration and communication between them is hard. Admittedly partly because of necessary security concerns. But also UI elements can look different. Keyboard short-cuts can clash. And size and shape constraints from both the original app and the host app are not necessarily compatible. Web design is, when you come to think of it, a surprisingly large amount of work, just trying to fit components that come from one app, together visually. Let alone two apps. And if you hoped to nest three apps, one inside another? And if your ideal is that end users can compose multiple apps together on the fly? That's why I personally believe in text. And it's one of the reasons I was excited when the original ChatGPT appeared. Not only because of the AI. But because a chat window seems to me to be the best way to get something closer to the Unix terminal experience out to a large number of mainstream users. If I were to sit down to try to create a new UI shell for a new operating system, it would start with a chatbot as the main front-end. Internally it would deal with something like the "Intents" you have on Android. Where multiple "apps" can register to handle the same data type or task. The chatbot would route natural language requests to these apps and replies in text. Sometimes the app might need to show ad hoc GUI components (eg a drawing canvas, a colour picker) as and when needed. Behind the scenes, the bot would manage installing and routing data between the apps; storing / publishing final results etc.
The moral imperative for composable systems alexanderobenauer.com

🚀 Exploring SystemVerilog Enums! 🚀 Today, I dived into SystemVerilog enumerations (enums) and had some fun experimenting with their built-in methods. Enums allow us to define a set of named values instead of relying on raw numbers — which makes designs more readable and maintainable. Here’s what I explored: .first() & .last() → Access the first and last enum elements .num() → Count the number of elements in the enum .name() → Get the name of an enum element .next() & .prev() → Traverse forward and backward through enum elements *Code:* module tb; typedef enum{red, blue, green, yellow, white, black} my_enum; my_enum vs; my_enum temp; initial begin $display("--------------------------------------------------------"); $display("value of first element of enum = %0d \n", vs.first()); $display("value of last element of enum = %0d \n", vs.last()); $display("number of element in enum = %0d \n", vs.num()); $display("name of enum = %0s \n", vs.name()); vs = green; $display("next index value of green = %0d \n", vs.next()); $display("previous index value of green = %0d \n", vs.prev()); $display("elements of enum are :"); temp = vs.first(); do begin $write("%s, ", temp.name()); temp = temp.next(); end while(temp != vs.first()); $display("\n"); temp = white; temp = temp.next(); $display("next element of white is = %0s \n", temp.name()); vs = vs.prev(); $display("previous element of green is = %0s \n", vs.name()); $display("--------------------------------------------------------"); end endmodule I even looped through all enum elements using a do-while loop, which is super handy for iterating without manually keeping track of indexes. ✅ Why this matters: Enums improve code readability, reduce bugs from magic numbers, and allow clean iteration over states or modes in your digital designs. Feeling excited to apply these in finite state machines (FSMs) and complex RTL designs!#SystemVerilog#VLSI#DigitalDesign#FPGA#RTLDesign#HardwareVerification#Engineering
5mo
The Internet is Broken and the Way We Build It is to Blame We are in an age where we have AI powerful enough to write code, clouds that scale infinitely and hardware that can handle billions of operations per second. Yet somehow, the average website is broken, leaks user data, burns through CPU and breaks on half the browsers. The problem isn't the internet or AI. It's the culture around how we build. We've layered convenience over craftsmanship. We obsess over frameworks, not fundamentals. We chase deadlines over durability. We treat software like disposable fashion, not infrastructure. The internet wasn't meant to be a bloated maze of APIs, JS bundles and UI libraries stitched together with duct tape. It was meant to be fast, open and resilient and a space where engineering meant elegance, not excess. Good engineering is invisible. Bad engineering is everywhere, in every spinning loader, every memory leak, every "please refresh the page" moment that reminds us just how fragile the modern web has become. Maybe it's not the engineers. Maybe it's the pressure to ship, not shape. And somewhere in that rush, the internet lost a bit of its soul.

While most LLMs still use the same transformer backbone, Liquid AI’s LFM2 models take a different route. They’re designed from the ground up for speed and efficiency, not just scale. Here’s what stands out 👇 1️⃣ Hybrid architecture – Instead of relying purely on self-attention, LFM2 blends short-range convolution blocks with grouped-query attention, reducing computation without losing context. 2️⃣ Smaller, faster models – Sizes range from 350 M to 8 B parameters, but training and inference are 2–3× faster than regular transformers of similar size. 3️⃣ Optimized for CPU and edge – These models run efficiently even without high-end GPUs, making them viable for local or on-device deployment. 4️⃣ Better efficiency curves – Each added parameter gives more performance gain per compute unit — that’s their real innovation. In short: > LFM2 models aren’t about being smarter than transformers — they’re about being smarter with compute. Explore the full Liquid AI Cookbook here → https://lnkd.in/gmffu5Hz

When we started the Frameworks for Growth interview series, I wanted to have real conversations with the people building and backing today’s fastest-growing companies—and extract the frameworks that make growth repeatable, not lucky. Over the first five episodes, I got to learn from: * Garry Tan, who reminded us that persistence is underrated (especially in an era of swooping revenue growth curves?) * Daniela Amodei, who shared how trust can coexist with velocity and what it takes to build enduring culture when the world is watching every move you make * Amjad Masad who built Replit by betting that the next billion developers won’t look like the last * Bret Taylor, who explained why AI is still in its Yahoo! Directory phase * Steffen Tjerrild, who gave the case for building a world-class AI company in Europe Building is always hard, AI is changing everything, and the frameworks for how we decide, prioritize, and scale matter more than ever. Huge thanks to these founders for their candor, and to everyone who’s listened, shared, and debated along the way.

It's often easier to grow a company from $100 billion to $200 billion than it is to pick the breakout company from a few thousand. A decade ago, there were virtually no trillion-dollar companies, and the largest tech companies were valued at $3-500 billion. Today, we have ten companies in the trillion-dollar range, with the top 3-4 valued at $3-4 trillion. This shift highlights the increasing power of AI technology and its impact on company valuations. The analysis shows that picking the 75 companies that reached $100 billion from a few hundred is a much better bet than trying to find the unicorns that will reach $10 billion. Totally nailing automation and AI isn't just about picking the shiny new tech toy. 🤖 According to Brandon Sammut, CPO of Zapier, it's mostly a people problem, not a technology problem. Everyone's deep in the weeds debating the latest LLM (and we get why), but the truth is the companies actually winning the AI game aren't those with the fanciest tech stack. They're the ones where the team feels safe to try new things, mess up a little, and share the lessons learned. So, before you overhaul your organization's tech, it's time to look inward. 🧘♂️ → Why does this matter for YOUR organization? → How will YOUR people benefit? → What's YOUR authentic story? The companies winning at AI aren't the ones with the best tech stack. They're the ones where people feel safe to experiment, fail, and share what they learned. Before you transform your tech, transform your culture. 🪄 🎧 Listen to the full episode of 'From a People Perspective' featuring Brandon: https://lnkd.in/gudwFWpW If you had to pick one culture habit to ditch right now to foster more experimentation, what would it be? 👇

Some engineering principles I live by, learned over the years at Vercel: ✓ Make it work, make it right, make it fast ✓ Progressive disclosure of complexity ✓ Minimize the number of concepts & modes ✓ Most 'flukes' aren't… your tech just sucks ✓ Feedback must be given to users instantly ✓ Maximize user exposure hours ✓ Demo your software frequently to fresh eyes ✓ Sweat every word of product copy you render ✓ You're never done working on performance ✓ You're never done. Software ages like milk, not wine ✓ Visualizing traces of time is the best way to optimize it ✓ Ship frequently and strive to build in public ✓ Errors must have globally unique codes & hyperlinks ✓ Red is not enough to signal "error" (8% of men have red-green color blindness)

If performance is critical to a system, performance needs to be an integral part of the design. It doesn't mean that all code should be optimized from the beginning, but it should be clear that the initial design can meet the performance criteria. Writing a huge system and then starting to measure/optimize could mean a full rewrite. In high-throughput or low-latency systems, you typically start with a basic core that meets the performance criteria. With the core in place, new functionality can be added and further optimizations can be done where needed.

Here are mine: - Know your tools and build your own for repeated workflows. - Done is better than perfect. - Write code that's easy to replace, not easy to extend. - Develop. Preview. Ship. Repeat. - Premature optimization is the root of all evil. - Read the Fucking Manual (RTFM). - Documentation Driven Development (DDD). - Open Source All The Things! - Some programmers are 10x more efficient than others. I know because I've been both a 10x programmer and a -1x programmer. - You may think that software development at a software company serves the customer directly, but you’d be mistaken. The organisation is the software development team’s primary customer

The "Blueprint" - From "Idea" to "Specification" What’s better than a 100-page spec document? A spec you can actually run. Every chip starts as an "Idea." But to become a product, that idea must be evaluated (market fit, technical feasibility) and turned into a solid "Specification." This spec defines features, power, performance, and cost (PPA). This is where Model-Based System Engineering (MBSE) shines. Instead of static documents, we build executable models that become the "single source of truth," allowing us to validate our assumptions. A "runnable" spec ensures all teams (HW, SW, and validation) are building to the same, validated plan before the expensive design work starts. Have you ever been caught by a vague or outdated spec? How could an "executable" model have helped?

My problem with "software engineering" is that, to me, "engineering" suggests an activity that's grounded in empirically tested predictive models that are built on known causal mechanisms. We don't just predict that e.g., a function will have a higher likely failure rate because it's more complex, we have a pretty good idea *why*, too. (The ratio in the state space of ways it can be wrong / ways it can be right increases, so the probability of it being wrong goes up - basically, we make the goal's mouth smaller.) And, TBF, most teams aren't even predicting it. They just write the code and judge it on "feels". I think we *aspire* to engineering, but we're a long way off.

Sometimes we stand stuck in front of a closed door, not noticing there’s no wall beside it. That’s where TRIZ can help IT engineers find a way forward. In the 1960s, engineers developed TRIZ - the Theory of Inventive Problem Solving. It was based on the analysis of ~40,000 patents. Over time, TRIZ has been adapted to solve non-material and abstract problems as well. Why is this relevant for IT? Because IT systems are engineering systems. Just like engines or power grids, they evolve according to certain principles and TRIZ provides a structured framework for creative problem-solving, derived from patterns in thousands of patents. It’s highly applicable to software architecture. TRIZ in Software Architecture Lets review typical scenario: monolith application refactoring without moving to microservices. TRIZ shows us there’s no wall beside it. The goal is to improve maintainability, scalability, and development speed without the distributed complexity. Here’s how TRIZ principles guide the solution: 1. Segmentation: break the monolith into a modular monolith with enforced boundaries. 2. Local Quality: allow each module to use the most appropriate technology for its specific job. 3. Preliminary Action: build a robust CI/CD pipeline and use feature toggles to make deployments safe and fast. 4. The Other Way Around: invert the database dependency. Give modules private databases and make them communicate via APIs, not direct SQL. 5. Dynamicity: implement internal circuit breakers and bulkheads so modules can handle failure gracefully without bringing down the whole system. The result? A "TRIZ-Refactored" monolith that is segmented, technologically flexible, resilient, and ready for independent development - all within a single deployable unit. TRIZ gives engineers a powerful lens to solve technical contradictions and evolve systems intelligently. It’s not just about mechanics; it’s about fundamental inventive principles. Altshuller Institute for TRIZ studies: https://www.aitriz.org/#TRIZ#SoftwareArchitecture#SoftwareEngineering#Refactoring#SystemDesign

🚀 What If Lab Data Could Warn Us Before a Cracking Furnace Trips? n8n is an open-source, low-code workflow automation tool that lets you connect apps, APIs, and data sources without writing complex code. Think of it as digital LEGO for engineers: drag, drop, and link your systems together to automate repetitive or time-sensitive tasks. I’m a process engineer working on FHinfinity, our software for designing and simulating industrial cracking furnaces. While I’m not a full-time software developer, I work closely with our dev team—and that’s given me a front-row seat to how powerful lightweight automation can be when it meets real engineering challenges. Here’s a scenario that really sparked my imagination:🤔 Every day, fuel composition reports arrive from the lab via email. Right now, someone has to manually check if the new fuel mix might cause issues. (Such as HIGH HYDROGEN content of fuel produced in upstream units) But what if… 🔹 n8n automatically reads the incoming email, 🔹 Extracts key values (like H₂, CH₄, or sulfur content), 🔹 Sends them to a lightweight FHinfinity evaluation API (Burner Analyzer tool), 🔹 And if the simulation predicts high fuel pressure or combustion instability… 🔹 It instantly alerts the operations team: “⚠️ Warning: With today’s fuel mix, FHinfinity predicts fuel pressure will exceed safe limits. Risk of furnace trip. Review before startup.” No more waiting for a surprise shutdown. No more spreadsheet cross-checks. Just smart, proactive engineering—powered by simple automation. As someone who helps shape furnace simulation tools, I’m fascinated by how tools like n8n could turn FHinfinity into a real-time guardian, not just a design environment and I hope we can build it soon because it seems that all the pieces are already there: 📧 Email parsing → 📊 FHinfinity logic → 💬 Teams alert → ✅ Preventive action.#n8n#ProcessEngineering#FHinfinity#FurnaceDesign#IndustrialAutomation#DigitalTransformation#LowCode#LabToPlant#EngineeringInnovation#PreventiveEngineering
